clinical_history: Abnormal ECG in a middle-aged, acyanotic woman. The findings are most consistent with which ONE of the following diagnoses?
diagnosis: The ECG shows sinus rhythm with classic findings of severe right ventricular hypertrophy (RVH) consistent with a sustained pressure load. Note the combination of a tall R wave in V1 with marked right axis deviation (normal QRS duration), along with tall peaked P waves due to right atrial overload. This triad is specific for marked right ventricular hypertrophy due to a pressure load with either severe pulmonary artery hypertension or pulmonic stenosis. Note also the delayed precordial transition zone and terminal S waves in the left chest leads as part of this pattern. There are non-specific ST-T abnormalities with slight T wave inversions in the right chest leads consistent with right ventricular overload in this context. Note that the P wave is quite negative in V1this finding simulates left atrial abnormality and has to do with the exploring electrode being placed over a large right atrium that is depolarizing away from the positive pole of that lead. The narrowness of the P waves in the limb leads supports this contention.  The patient had recurrent pulmonary emboli. Similar findings might be seen with pulmonary hypertension and RVH from other causes, including Eisenmengers syndrome, primary pulmonary hypertension, or end-stage pulmonary restrictive lung disease.
